---
title: "Geocomputation with R"
subtitle: "Geographic vector data with sf"
author: "Jannes Muenchow, Jakub Nowosad"
date: 2026-07-06
---

## Setup

Slides and code: <https://github.com/geocompx/user26>

```{r}
#| eval: false
install.packages(c("sf", "spData", "dplyr"))
```

```{r}
library(sf)
library(spData) # example datasets: world, nz, nz_height, us_states
library(dplyr)
```

## The vector data model

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
::: {.incremental}
- Discrete objects with well-defined borders.
- Three main geometry types: **points**, **lines** and **polygons**.
- Especially suitable for objects with sharp boundaries (lakes, roads, houses).
- Geometries carry an **attribute table**.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
![](/img/vector.png)
:::
:::

Further reading: <https://r.geocompx.org/spatial-class#vector-data>

# Simple features in R {.section-divider}

## Simple features in R

Simple feature access is a widely used [ISO standard](https://www.ogc.org/standard/sfa/).
Edzer Pebesma implemented it in the **sf** package [@pebesma_simple_2018].

```{r}
library(sf)
```

`sf` automatically links to [GEOS](https://libgeos.org/),
[GDAL](https://gdal.org/) and [PROJ](https://proj.org/).

. . .

```{r}
class(nz)
```

An `sf` object is a **`data.frame`** (an S3 object) with a geometry column.

## An sf object is a data frame

```{r}
nz
```

## Plotting an sf object

```{r}
#| fig-height: 4.5
plot(nz)
```

## Plotting just the geometry

```{r}
#| fig-height: 4.5
plot(st_geometry(nz))
```

## sf works with the tidyverse

```{r}
select(nz, Name, Population) |> head(2)
```

- **sf** works with the **tidyverse**.
- Geometry is **just another column**.
- The geometry column is **sticky** (kept unless dropped explicitly).

## Notes on the geometry column

- Each row (feature) has one geometry, which may contain multiple parts
  (e.g. multipolygons, polygons with holes).

. . .

- The geometry column is a so-called **list-column**.

. . .

- The geometry is built from **simple** R structures (vectors, matrices, lists).

## Building geometries by hand

```{r}
#| fig-height: 3.8
# a point is a numeric vector
p <- st_point(c(1.25, 1.25))
# a line is a matrix of >= 2 points
l <- st_linestring(matrix(c(1.5, 1.5, 1.7, 1.7), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE))
# a polygon is a list of matrices
mat <- matrix(c(1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)
poly <- st_polygon(list(mat))
plot(poly)
plot(p, pch = 16, col = "blue", cex = 2, add = TRUE)
plot(l, col = "orange", lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
```

## Three classes: sf, sfc, sfg

```{r}
lc <- st_geometry(nz) # the geometry list-column
class(lc)
```

. . .

```{r}
class(lc[[1]]) # a single feature geometry
```

- `sf`  — the data frame (attributes + geometry list-column)
- `sfc` — the geometry list-column
- `sfg` — a single feature geometry

See `vignette("sf1", package = "sf")`.

# Attribute operations {.section-divider}

## Attribute operations

`sf` objects are data frames, so they behave like any other data frame.

```{r}
dim(nz)
```

. . .

```{r}
# first 2 rows and first 2 columns
nz[1:2, 1:2]
```

## Tidyverse verbs

The one-table **dplyr** verbs work: `select()`, `filter()`, `mutate()`,
`arrange()`, `summarise()` and `group_by()`, plus the pipe `|>`.

```{r}
nz |>
  filter(Island == "North") |>
  select(Name, Population) |>
  head(2)
```

Further reading: <https://r.geocompx.org/attr#vector-attribute-manipulation>

## Your turn

1. Select all regions of `nz` with a `Population` greater than 200,000. Plot the
   geometry of all regions and add your selection in another colour.
2. Add a categorical column `pop_class` to `nz`: `"low"` for populations below
   100,000, `"medium"` for 100,000–300,000, and `"high"` above.
3. *Optional:* create two points of class `sfg`, and combine them into an `sf`
   object with an `id` and a `geometry` column.

# Spatial attribute operations {.section-divider}

## Spatial attribute operations

Spatial operations make use of the spatial relationship between features:

- Spatial subsetting
- Topological / neighbourhood relations
- Spatial joins

## Spatial subsetting

```{r}
#| fig-height: 4
plot(st_geometry(nz))
plot(st_geometry(nz_height), pch = 16, col = "red", add = TRUE)
```

## Spatial subsetting

```{r}
#| fig-height: 3.8
canterbury <- nz |> filter(Name == "Canterbury")
# keep only the summits located inside Canterbury
canterbury_height <- nz_height[canterbury, ]
plot(st_geometry(canterbury))
plot(st_geometry(canterbury_height), pch = 16, col = "red", add = TRUE)
```

## Topological relations

Subsetting implicitly used `st_intersects()`. We can call it directly — it
returns which features intersect:

```{r}
sel <- st_intersects(nz_height, canterbury, sparse = FALSE)
head(sel)
```

. . .

Other predicates: `st_disjoint()`, `st_touches()`, `st_within()`,
`st_contains()`, ... — see `?st_intersects`.

## Spatial joins

Transfer attributes from one object to another based on their geometries.
Add the region `Name` from `nz` to each summit in `nz_height`:

```{r}
join <- st_join(nz_height, select(nz, Name))
join |>
  select(elevation, Name) |>
  head(2)
```

Further reading: <https://r.geocompx.org/spatial-operations#spatial-vec>

## Your turn

1. Find all summits in `nz_height` that do **not** intersect the Canterbury
   region.
2. What happens if you spatially join the `elevation` column of `nz_height`
   onto `nz` (i.e. `st_join(nz, nz_height)`)? How many rows result, and why?

# Geometric operations {.section-divider}

## Geometric operations

What if we want the geometric **intersection** of two overlapping objects,
rather than a logical vector?

![](/img/venn-clip-1.png){width="85%" fig-align="center"}

## Intersection and union

```{r}
#| fig-height: 3.6
pts <- st_sfc(st_point(c(0, 1)), st_point(c(1, 1)))
circles <- st_buffer(pts, dist = 1)
x <- circles[1]
y <- circles[2]
plot(st_union(x, y), border = "grey")
plot(st_intersection(x, y), col = "lightblue", add = TRUE)
```

## Spatial aggregation (dissolving)

```{r}
#| fig-height: 3.6
regions <- us_states |>
  group_by(REGION) |>
  summarise(pop = sum(total_pop_15, na.rm = TRUE))
plot(regions["pop"])
```

`summarise()` on an `sf` object dissolves the geometries per group.

## Coordinate reference systems

`sf` handles CRSs and reprojection through PROJ.

```{r}
st_crs(nz)$Name
```

. . .

Reproject with `st_transform()` (EPSG:4326 = geographic WGS84):

```{r}
nz_wgs <- st_transform(nz, crs = 4326)
st_crs(nz_wgs)$Name
```

Further reading: <https://r.geocompx.org/reproj-geo-data>

## Your turn

1. Create two overlapping circles (as above) and compute **and plot** their
   geometric intersection, then their union.
2. Compute the average population (`total_pop_15`) for each `REGION` of
   `us_states` and plot the result.
3. Find the CRS of `nz`, reproject it to EPSG:4326, and plot the original next
   to the transformed object.

## Recap

With `sf` objects we performed:

- **Attribute** operations (data-frame / dplyr verbs)
- **Spatial attribute** operations (subsetting, topological relations, joins)
- **Geometric** operations (intersection, union, dissolve, reprojection)

Next: **raster data with `terra`**.

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
